tasks.prompt: Warm-up: Add a Docker Compose healthcheck to the docker-compose.yml on the EC2 server, then run 'docker compose up -d --wait' manually and observe the output — confirming that '--wait' blocks until the healthcheck reports healthy.
tasks.starter:
# SSH into your EC2 instance and update docker-compose.yml:
cat > /home/ec2-user/docker-compose.yml << 'EOF'
services:
  web:
    image: ${IMAGE}
    ports:
      - "80:8080"
    restart: always
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8080/health"]
      interval: ___s
      timeout: ___s
      retries: ___
EOF

# Then deploy manually to test the --wait flag:
export IMAGE=<your-ecr-registry>/myapp:<last-commit-sha>
docker compose -f /home/ec2-user/docker-compose.yml up -d --wait
tasks.solution:
# docker-compose.yml with healthcheck:
cat > /home/ec2-user/docker-compose.yml << 'EOF'
services:
  web:
    image: ${IMAGE}
    ports:
      - "80:8080"
    restart: always
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8080/health"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 3
EOF

# Note: your app needs a /health endpoint for the healthcheck to work.
# Add this to index.js:
# if (req.url === '/health') { res.writeHead(200); res.end('OK'); return; }

# Run with --wait:
export IMAGE=123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/myapp:abc1234
docker compose -f /home/ec2-user/docker-compose.yml up -d --wait

# Expected output:
# [+] Running 1/1
#  ✔ Container ec2-user-web-1  Healthy
# (Returns only after the healthcheck passes, exits 0)

# If the /health endpoint does not exist:
# The command waits up to (interval * retries) = 30 seconds, then exits non-zero:
# Error response from daemon: ... health check failed
tasks.explanation: '--wait' turns 'docker compose up -d' from a fire-and-forget command into a verified deployment: it polls the healthcheck probe at the configured interval, up to retries times, and only exits 0 if the container transitions to 'healthy' within that budget. A fixed 'sleep 10' cannot do this — it races the app's startup time and gives no useful exit code. The healthcheck test command ('curl -f http://localhost:8080/health') must succeed (exit 0) for the container to be considered healthy; '-f' makes curl exit non-zero on HTTP errors.
hints: If 'curl' is not available inside the container (alpine image), use 'wget -q --spider http://localhost:8080/health' or 'node -e "require(http).get(url, r => process.exit(r.statusCode===200?0:1))"' as the healthcheck test command.
If 'docker compose up -d --wait' exits immediately with an error about '--wait', your Compose version may be older than v2.1. Check 'docker compose version' — you need v2.1+.

tasks.prompt: Core practice: Update the 'Deploy to EC2' stage in your Jenkinsfile to implement the health-check-plus-rollback pattern. The stage should: deploy with '--wait', write the tag to 'last_good_tag' on success, and roll back to the saved tag in a 'post { failure { ... } }' block if '--wait' exits non-zero.
tasks.explanation: The self-correction mechanism works because of two design choices: (1) 'echo ${commit} > last_good_tag' runs only AFTER '--wait' exits 0, so the file always contains a verified, healthy tag — a failed deploy never overwrites it; (2) 'post { failure { ... } }' runs only when the stage fails, giving the rollback block access to the last verified tag. This creates a safety net without any external state management: the EC2 server itself stores the rollback reference in a plain text file. The backslash escaping ('\\$PREV') is required because the outer Groovy string interpolation must be prevented from consuming the shell variable before the SSH session sees it.
hints: If the 'post { failure { ... } }' block does not run when the deploy fails, verify that the 'docker compose up -d --wait' failure propagates an exit code through the SSH call. Add 'set -e' at the start of the remote shell script to ensure any command failure exits the script.
If 'last_good_tag' is empty on first run (no previous successful deploy), the rollback correctly prints the 'No known-good tag found' message and exits 1 — that is intentional behaviour, not a bug.

To deliberately trigger a rollback for testing: push a broken image (e.g. a Dockerfile that sets CMD to 'exit 1') and watch '--wait' time out, the stage turn red, and the post block deploy the previous tag.

tasks.prompt: Core practice: Implement safe secret management for the pipeline. Store a mock database password as a Jenkins credential and a runtime application secret in a '.env' file on the EC2 server. Verify the Jenkins build log does NOT show the secret value, and confirm the running container can access the runtime secret as an environment variable.
tasks.explanation: The two-tier secret model separates concerns cleanly: Jenkins credentials store secrets the pipeline needs at deploy time (for example, to authenticate to an API during deployment); the '.env' file on EC2 stores secrets the running application needs at runtime (database passwords, API keys). Jenkins' 'withCredentials' wrapper masks the secret in logs by scanning all output and replacing the value with '****'. The '.env' file never enters Git (it is in '.gitignore' on the server) and never passes through Jenkins — it exists only on the server that actually runs the app. 'chmod 600' restricts the file so only the ec2-user can read it.
hints: If 'withCredentials' still shows the secret in the log, check that the variable name in the 'sh' command matches the 'variable' field exactly — case-sensitive.
If the container cannot see environment variables from the .env file, verify that 'env_file' in docker-compose.yml points to the absolute path (not a relative path from where you run docker compose).

Never use 'docker run -e DB_PASSWORD=<value>' with a literal secret in the pipeline command — it would appear in 'docker inspect' output and the build log. The 'env_file' approach keeps the value only in the file, not in any command string.

tasks.prompt: Applied challenge: Deliberately trigger the rollback mechanism. Introduce a bug that will cause the healthcheck to fail (for example, make the /health endpoint return HTTP 500 or make the app crash on startup), push it, and confirm Jenkins: (1) runs '--wait' and times out, (2) marks the Deploy stage red, (3) executes the rollback post block, and (4) leaves the last known-good version running on EC2.
tasks.explanation: This challenge is the definitive end-to-end test of the safety net. Three outcomes confirm everything is working correctly: (1) the Deploy stage turns red — proving '--wait' is doing real verification, not just waiting an arbitrary time; (2) last_good_tag is unchanged — proving the file is only written on verified success; (3) the service is still serving the previous version — proving automatic rollback worked without human intervention. Restoring a broken service automatically, even when the pipeline itself is the one that detected the problem, is the production-readiness feature that justifies all the setup effort.
hints: If the Deploy stage turns green even with the broken healthcheck, confirm that 'docker-compose.yml' has the 'healthcheck' section — without it, '--wait' only waits for the container to *start*, not to become *healthy*, so it exits 0 regardless.
If the rollback block also fails (because 'last_good_tag' does not exist on first run), that is expected behaviour — push a successful build first to create the file, then introduce the break.
If you want to shorten the wait during testing, temporarily set 'retries: 1' in the healthcheck to fail after 10 seconds instead of 30.
